conflicts: Refund handling differs (some include refunds, some don't)

definitions.name: monthly_revenue
definitions.description: Total revenue from completed orders, grouped by month. Excludes refunds and cancellations.
definitions.sql: SELECT DATE_TRUNC('month', order_date) AS month, SUM(amount) AS revenue FROM orders WHERE status = 'completed' GROUP BY 1

definitions.name: monthly_revenue
definitions.description: Revenue per month across all orders placed, including pending and refunded.
definitions.sql: SELECT DATE_TRUNC('month', order_date) AS month, SUM(amount) AS revenue FROM orders GROUP BY 1

definitions.name: revenue_monthly
definitions.description: Net monthly revenue after subtracting refunds, for completed and shipped orders.
definitions.sql: SELECT DATE_TRUNC('month', order_date) AS month, SUM(amount - refund_amount) AS revenue FROM orders WHERE status IN ('completed','shipped') GROUP BY 1

conflicts.recommendation:
Canonical definition (Finance glossary): net revenue = SUM(amount - refund_amount) for orders WHERE status = 'completed', grouped by calendar month.

Required changes per team:
• Finance — correct: already filters completed orders. Add refund subtraction: SUM(amount) → SUM(amount - refund_amount).
• Sales — remove all-orders scan; add WHERE status = 'completed' and subtract refunds. Current figure inflates revenue by the full refund amount.
• Marketing — remove 'shipped' from status filter; 'completed' only. Refund subtraction already present — keep it.

Executive impact: Finance and Sales dashboards currently diverge by the refund rate (typically 5–15%), making quarterly revenue reviews unreliable and growth forecasts inconsistent across leadership decks.

definitions.name: active_users
definitions.description: Distinct users with any event in the trailing 30 days.
definitions.sql: SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT user_id) FROM events WHERE event_date >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '30 days'

definitions.name: MAU
definitions.description: Monthly active users: distinct users active in the current calendar month.
definitions.sql: SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT user_id) FROM events WHERE DATE_TRUNC('month', event_date) = DATE_TRUNC('month', CURRENT_DATE)

definitions.name: monthly_active_users
definitions.description: Users who logged in at least once in the last 30 days. Only counts login events.
definitions.sql: SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT user_id) FROM events WHERE event_type = 'login' AND event_date >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '30 days'

conflicts.recommendation:
Canonical definition (Product Analytics glossary): distinct users with at least one qualifying event (ANY event, not only logins) in the current CALENDAR month.

Required changes per team:
• Product — switch from trailing-30-day window to calendar-month window. (CURRENT_DATE - 30 days) and (calendar month) diverge mid-month by up to 30 days.
• Growth — already uses calendar month. Verify all event types are counted, not just a subset; SQL looks correct.
• Data — remove event_type = 'login' restriction (MAU counts any engagement, not only logins) and switch to calendar-month window to match the standard.

Executive impact: Board-level MAU can show a 10–20% discrepancy between Product and Data teams — understating engagement in investor reports and mis-calibrating growth targets.

definitions.name: average_order_value
definitions.sql: SELECT SUM(amount) / COUNT(DISTINCT order_id) FROM orders WHERE status = 'completed'

definitions.name: AOV
definitions.description: Average order value net of refunds across all orders.
definitions.sql: SELECT SUM(amount - refund_amount) / COUNT(DISTINCT order_id) FROM orders

conflicts.recommendation:
Canonical definition (Finance glossary): AOV = SUM(amount - refund_amount) / COUNT(DISTINCT order_id) for completed orders only.

Required changes per team:
• Growth — add refund subtraction: SUM(amount) → SUM(amount - refund_amount). Filter to completed orders: add WHERE status = 'completed'.
• Finance — restrict denominator to completed orders: add WHERE status = 'completed' to exclude pending/cancelled orders from the order count.

Executive impact: AOV figures differ by the return rate (typically 3–8%), causing misaligned pricing decisions and GMV discussions between Finance and Growth in business reviews.
